### SCENE 26.19: Twilight pokes a hole in Coltbert's Theory
> **Synopsis:** Fleur feels scandalized that Twilight is disagreeing with her mentor, mirroring how Twilight felt scandalized from Fleur poking holes in Celestia's rule

**Characters:**
* **[[Coltbert]]**
> **Analysis:** his critical flaw as the Universal Ambition Myth
> he projects his own thrill-seeking personality onto the entire population and builds a system that has no place for the 25% Pink Population who simply want contentment and community. The Wallflowers are the structural proof that Ego-Capitalism, even when it is harmonic, assumes a universal drive that isn't actually universal.


**Threads:**
* **[[Twilight Breaking from Celestia]]**
> **Update:** In the ways that Fleur got Twilight to break from Celestia, now Twilight is getting Fleur to break from Coltbert!

* **[[Discovering the Elements of Liberty]]**
> **Update:** for most of the story, the Aquileian volunteers and the "Harmonic Capitalism" they brought with them to Equestria and the Crystal Empire is what saved the "little ponies" from Chrysalis. But once in Aquileia they realize the Aquileian version is ego-driven.
>
> Not because Coltbert is evil, but because he built a system that is genuinely better than Chrysalis's and genuinely better than Celestia's, and it still cannot account for the 25% who don't want to compete. His system assumes they are a rounding error. They are not.


**Codex:**
* **[[Earth Pony Magic]]** (MagicSystem)
> **Note:** She suggests that pride/ambition/ego (red love) are not the only catalysts for earth pony magic, and that grace, contentment and nurturing the land out of love (pink love) also works

* **[[Cutie Marks]]** (MagicSystem)
> **Note:** Coltbert says cutie marks a manifestation of special talents and they applied it to griffons
> Twilight counters with her cutie mark theory about the reversal of the cause and effect
> The cutie mark doesn't give the discount, the discount comes from your ambition, which gives the cutie mark
> Twilight then points to the fact that griffons change their seals when they move on. Some ponies also change their stamp designs, right? Aren't there some ponies who use a stamp for your "tableau" which doesn't match their cutie mark exactly?
>
> You are using the cutie mark and seals as "asset specificity" and "a guarentee of dignity" because you lack the moral framework to grant dignity to every creature regardless of their mark or seal, Applejack accused
>
> Coltbert counters by asking how does Celestia move the sun without crystals? It must be her cutie mark discount, there's no other explanation
> Twilight shrugs and says she moved the sun before


---
### SCENE 26.20: Henri meets Fleur in Pridea
> **Synopsis:** Applejack and SECEF volunteers return to Equestria. Henri meets Fleur at the University of Pridea. (Bonus chapter with a clop scene?) They recount how they met here and Fleur talks about how her parents let her go

---
### SCENE 26.21: The Origin of their Aquileian Partnership
> **Synopsis:** For Henri, my backstory for him talks about how he was once a thug, then once the reforms reach his town, he goes back to Pridea alone and apologizes to each farm and offers to fix something. This should attract the attention of all the Aquileian Lionesses in Pridea. He isn't tamed by anyone, he is tamed by morality. He's the ultimate eagle and a trophy. He doesn't accept any advances until he is done helping all the farms on his list. The last request is from Fleur's parents, who tell him to go help Fleur at the university. But Fleur is obsessed with research (like season 1 Twilight) and antisocial and has a long list of tasks to do. So this should be hillarious with a bunch of Lionesses trying to catch Henri's attention while he's moving boxes around a lab and the scientist is a nerd who isn't paying attention.
>
> Fleur and Henri should be frustrated that they aren't getting what the other is teaching. Fleur is frustrated about trying to see how magic will affect the battlefield, she doesn't want to think about it. Henri is frustrated about magic, he can't comprehend it (feeds into the theme about him be flabbergasted about each new form, like pegasi weather, then Tzinacatl rubber alchemy, then Spellblast tanks, then magical cas). When the frustration reaches a boiling point, they suddenly decide to relieve it sexually.
>
> No "Destiny": Just a griffon working off a debt and a mare stressed out by her thesis.
> 2.No "Romance": Just two ambitious people using sex to bypass a communication breakdown.
> 3.The Result: The relationship isn't the goal; the relationship is the engine that allows them to build the tech that saves the world.

---
### SCENE 26.22: Scene 2
> **Synopsis:** Another complementary Henri and Fleur tryst, where they swap lion/eagle roles, when Henri sees Fleur in Pridea after the Kemerskai party. Fleur can take advantage of the fact that Henri told a long speech to make a joke about "the posseur going soft" and that establishes their temporary roles. It can directly reference Henri's speech about defacing a pony's property, revealing that it was on Fleur's farm. (Are you going to apologize for breaking that? She asks, looming over him) After Gaudreau brought "Capitalism for good" to Henri's home town, he went back to Pridea to apologize for his stupid and vain actions. He offered to work in service to Fleur's parents to make up for it (this is organic, direct, personal service as opposed to GR's mandated community service for petty crimes). Fleur's parents told him to go help Fleur at the University of Pridea because she needs a griffon test subject for whatever she's working on. It should be clear that they were just friends for a long time (it's not an immediate lustful encounter) but as they worked together to plan Gaudreau's revolution in Pridea (Henri as a tactician and Fleur providing magic tech, precursor to magical combined arms) they naturally got intimate. Shared passion and ambition for the revolution. And that's just how Aquileian culture works.

---
### SCENE 26.23: Gaudreau informs Applejack of the urgency of why Aquileia needs Equestria to join the Republican Pact
> **Synopsis:** AJ and her friends meet in Pridea (or Aquila?)
> AJ talks to Gaudreau now
> Their conversation is a mix of Equestrian and Aquileian. It's a bit of struggle for both sides. Gaudreau knows some Equestrian from when she was with Coltbert, who visited Equestria, and also from talks with New Marelanders. AJ picked up from Aquileian from Henri and Fleur.
> This is a struggle, but it's also wholly authentic, as a counterpoint to the universal translator. (This is going to be hella hard to write but it will be worth it)
> If there is a genuine misunderstanding, Fleur or Henri or Comet Shine (Equestrian who learned Aquileian) are on standby to clear it up (and they will at times). But the point is, both AJ and Gaudreau choose to converse like this. Maybe Rarity will help too?
>
> AJ says she was initially scared the GR and Kemerskai would not be very good, but was proved wrong
> The GR probably thought Equestrian ponies are soft and were also proven wrong 
> Gaudreau now says in stark terms how important it is for AJ to succeed in turning Equestria into a Republic specifically modeled after the Aquileian system in order to signal that once the war with the Reich is over, Aquileia will be untouchable and the pan Griffonian dream will be tabled 
> If Equestria does not become a Republic modeled after Aquileia, then the future of the Republican Pact as a whole is uncertain, because the Aquileian citizens will never join a pan Griffonian Republic. Their shared values are ironclad, but their cultures are wholly incompatible 
> This is where Gaudreau helps AJ write the rules of the referendum
>
> Gaudreau needs Equestria to become a republic and join the alliance so that the GR cannot swallow Aquileia after victory
>
> Equestria already had local elections, they just needed to build up a central legistature and executive branch to replace Celestia and her court and also modernize the judiciary system
> They adopt many of the practices of the Aquileian Republic
>
> When setting up the parliament, the Aquileians say to give good salaries to the representatives
> Give them enough so they won't feel the need to be bribed and can attract talented folk who want to serve but otherwise couldn't without a good salary
> If someone wants to be a greedy bastard, they can try running a company. Never make incentive for election to be a financial opportunity 
> The communists may decry giving privilege to elected leaders but the reality is we must compete with companies
> There are many good, smart ponies who want to help run the country and represent their neighbors but if they can't support themselves that leads to possible corruption
>
> Gaudreau explains that the parties are not monoliths. Even her own party can oppose her legistation (refer to the in game tooltip "total obedience is a monarchist idea, anyway")
> So in Equestria there would not be monoliths either. There is individual freedom.
> The true pan griffonianists are only about 25% of the country, but it can go up over time if it's just Aquileia vs Herzland. They need a counterbalance.
> They owe their synthesis of harmony and capitalism to Equestria, since that's where Coltbert got it from
>
> Tall Tale: Applejack sees the "Idealized Republic" (FJA Volunteers working perfectly with Star Energy).
>
> The Real Twist: Applejack Meets "The Noise"
>
> The shock for Applejack isn't that the FJA is "losing"; it is that no one is actually winning.
>
>     Applejack's Perspective (Monarchist): She comes from Equestria. Even though she is "Honesty," she is used to Unity. When Celestia speaks, the country moves. There is one "Equestrian Way."
>
>     The Aquileian Reality (Republican): She arrives expecting to meet "The Leader" (Gaudreau or Vérany) who embodies the "Aquileian Spirit."
>
>         The Twist: She finds a room full of griffons and ponies screaming at each other.
>
>         The Lesson: Henri and Fleur didn't lie, but they simplified it. The "Aquileian Way" isn't the FJA platform; the "Aquileian Way" is the argument itself. It is the ceaseless, noisy, frustrating negotiation between the Soul (FJA) and the Machine (PNdA).
>
> The Narrative Beat: Applejack asks Henri: "Who's in charge here?" Henri laughs: "Everyone. And no one. That's what we fought for, Applejack. The right to argue."
>
> "Democracy is a process, not a destination" theme. The "Aquileian Way" isn't FJA policy; the Aquileian Way is the fight between FJA and PNdA.
>
> The Monarchist Expectation: Applejack comes from a land where, if you are the Ruler (Celestia), you have absolute power. She expects Gaudreau—the "Princess of the Republic"—to simply decree that the FJA way is the law.
>
> The Republican Reality: The shock comes when Applejack sees Gaudreau, the "Winner," begging Vérany (the "Loser") for votes to pass a budget.
>
> The Lesson: It proves that Democracy is not about "Ruling"; it is about "Herding Cats." Even with the Presidency, the FJA cannot stop the slide toward the Griffonian Republic (GR) without help. This validates your "Twist #1" perfectly: FJA is in charge, but they are powerless to stop the cultural drift without outside validation (Equestria).
>
> The Griffonian Republic's Gravity: The GR claims to be the legitimate government of all Griffons. It is a massive, centralized, industrial machine (Herzlander culture + Cloudbury resources). Culturally, it exerts a massive pull.
> •The Aquileian Fear: Without a counterweight, Aquileia risks becoming a "Satellite State" or "Junior Partner." The GR might eventually say, "We are all Griffons; why do you need a separate army/currency? Join the Federation."
> If Equestria is a Republic: It becomes a Superpower Validator. Suddenly, the "Harmonic Model" isn't just a minority Aquileian quirk; it is the ideology of the strongest economy in the world
> The Universal Translator is magnificent, but it's also a threat

**Characters:**
* **[[Applejack]]**
> **Analysis:** Applejack represents the perfect success story of the revolution
> She started as a monarchist general by appointment (not merit). She was chosen for celebrity status, not competence. Her loss of self-esteem when faced with professional changelings was the death of the stagnation of harmony.
>
> She didn't regain hope from a speech from the princess, in fact she had to give the rallying speech after Princess Luna ran.
>
> Henri and Fleur slowly taught Applejack to become the face of the new Republic
> Now you must go back home and enact that vision, reform into the Equestrian Republic and join the Republican Pact. Then Aquileia will never fall to Herzland
>
> Twilight says, so you were conditioning her, like how Celestia conditioned me?
> Well yes... but Presidents have term limits.
> Twilight gets annoyed at how the Aquileians treat them like their children just like Celestia. It makes them look in the mirror at their own cultural superiority complex and humble themselves. After they apologize, Applejack does accept this burden of repayment to save her friends from ultimate loss of identity
> I already have to repay war bonds, calm down bloodthirsty ponies, liberate the changeling lands, fix Skyfall, what harm does adding "Save Aquileia" to my to do list bring? Applejack chuckled?

* **[[Henri Gourard]]**
> **Analysis:** The 1st Revolution didn't offer his parents dignity; it offered them factory shifts and attempted to erase his local culture. His region broke off and turned to Supremacy/Monarchism because the "Republic" felt like a foreign occupation by city griffons.
>
> Admittedly, Moriset Discret changed Aquileia forever by letting Coltbert import "Equestrian" ideas in his reforms
>
> Henri is so passionate because the FJA is the only entity that ever gave his kind dignity. Really, he's been embracing the "Equestrian pony way" all along
>
> He didn't mean to "condition" Applejack. He was there to help Applejack scale up her values (harmonic capitalism)


**Threads:**
* **[[Perception of the Griffonian Republic]]** (Negative)

**Themes:**
* **[[Loyalty and Kinship]]**
> **Commentary:** Twilight's directive to be the Princess was based on Fealty (following Celestia's orders for validation - fuels anxiety). Applejack's directive to become the President is based on Fraternity (suggestion and consent of peers)
> Applejack has a choice to accept this burden, and she takes it. Twilight didn't have a choice, and ultimately rejected it.


**Codex:**
* **[[Aquileian and Herzlander Cultural Differences]]** (SocietalDifferences)
> **Note:** The Argument: The Griffonian Republic thinks Aquileia is Naive. "You rely on everyone being nice. What happens when they aren't?"
> •The Counter: Aquileia thinks the GR is Cynical. "You legislate morality instead of teaching it."
> •Equestria's Role: Applejack proves you need Both. You need the Aquileian Heart (Values) protected by the Griffonian Shield (Institutions).


---
### SCENE 26.24: Twilight Unveils the Elements of Liberty to Celestia
> **Synopsis:** Twilight dressing up their grand synthesis in the vocabulary of the nursery in order to pitch it to Celestia. So Twilight goes on a full on parallel outlining the five war time elements and says they found the sixth hidden element that binds the other 5 in Griffonia, the Element of Conscience.
> In Friendship is Magic part 2, Twilight's long animé style declaration was addressed towards Nightmare Moon (and the audience, the watchers of the cartoon). Here, Twilight's declaration is to Celestia who is the apparent domestic antagonist (and addressed to the readers).
>
> Each canon element contains a hidden assumption that makes it work in Equestria's nursery and fail in the real world:
>
> Kindness assumes the other party has some capacity to receive it — that something in them can be reached
> This was impossible against conscripts who were drugged to have no emotion sense
>
> Generosity assumes the other party operates in good faith and won't simply extract - friendship seminars and the gold standard had no defense against rugged individualism and lying, ponies who chose greed over friends
>
> Loyalty assumes the bond is mutual — that your loyalty isn't being weaponized
> But this turns into an unhealthy and unsustainable guilt when your loyalty is to an abstract, the entire army, the entire state
> Twilight cites her attempt to fix Ain Trotgourait and Rainbow Dash flying until she couldn't fly anymore
>
> Laughter assumes shared humanity — that joy is a language everyone speaks
> This does not work against predators who laugh at hurting you, or conscripts who are drugged
>
> Honesty assumes the other party has a conscience to appeal to
> At Ain Trotgourait, behind Twilight's shield, Applejack's Honesty was worthless against warlords with no moral compass. Not because she was wrong, but because Honesty's power is entirely dependent on the listener's conscience. Truth lands only when there is something inside the listener that can receive it and feel its weight. Remove that, and Honesty becomes noise.
>
> Elements of war, we had to suppress what we learned to face an enemy we weren't prepared for to match them
> Decreaturization, personal sacrifice, ignoring our friends' pleas to stay safe, unleashing our firepower and letting go of naive hope and turning grim
>
> But we found a better way
>
> Grace prevents conscience from being destroyed by dehumanization (the Nazi/Imperial mechanism)
> Equity prevents conscience from being crowded out by extraction (the Capitalist mechanism)
> Kinship prevents conscience from being atomized by surveillance and siloing (the Big Tech/KGB mechanism)
> Ambition prevents conscience from being starved of the capacity to act (the Stagnation mechanism)
> Resilience prevents conscience from being numbed by repeated trauma (the Total War mechanism)
>
> These allowed us to defend Equestria, but you stopped us because it would not lead to peace
>
> Conscience itself is not one of these defense mechanisms. It is what they all defend. It is the variable — the internal commitment — that makes the other five elements into a stable equilibrium rather than a strategic calculation.
> This is why it is hidden until all five are present. You cannot demonstrate Conscience in isolation because Conscience in isolation is just another word for goodwill, which is what the Nursery assumed it already had. Conscience becomes visible as a distinct force — as the sixth element — only when it has survived all five mechanisms designed to destroy it and remained intact. At that point it is no longer ambient or assumed. It has been chosen, defended, and demonstrated under conditions that would have justified its abandonment.
> When Twilight names it in the declaration, she is not identifying a new virtue. She is naming the thing they all proved they still had after everything that tried to take it. The war was the cost of demonstrating Conscience. The republic is what you build once you've paid it.
>
> In FiM, "the element of magic existed in the heart of us all" means: the capacity for friendship, for genuine connection, was always latent in Twilight — the bookworm, the recluse, the one who said she didn't need friends. It just needed conditions to emerge.
> In TLTT, "Conscience existed in the heart of us all" means something more historically specific and more painful: Equestria was built on it. The Stagnation of Harmony was not just compatible with conscience — it was the result of an act of collective conscience, the ponies who saw what the Griffonian Gilded Age did to griffons and chose differently. But because Celestia's Nursery protected conscience by removing all adversity, the conscience it protected was never tested, never strengthened, never made durable. It existed in the heart of all Equestrian ponies but couldn't defend itself.
> The war didn't give the ponies a conscience they lacked. It proved that the conscience they always had was strong enough to survive adversity — and then it built the republic, which is the first system in their history designed to cultivate and protect conscience without removing the adversity that makes it real.
> This is the inversion of the naive assumption. Celestia didn't lack the insight that conscience matters. She lacked the belief that it could survive the test. The republic is Applejack saying: it can. We just proved it.
>
> every system they've encountered, including the best ones, still depends on an assumption it cannot guarantee. Celestia assumed ponies were good. Coltbert assumed ponies were ambitious. Chrysalis stopped assuming anything and just removed the variable. The only thing none of them built was the thing that makes any system legitimate at all: the conditions for citizens to have and exercise their own conscience.
>
> Celestia is the embodiment of the world that assumed conscience — who built the Nursery precisely because she knew the external world would destroy it. The declaration being addressed to her is Twilight saying: you were right that conscience is fragile. You were wrong that the answer is to protect it by removing all adversity. The answer is to build conditions that make it durable. This is the only argument that could actually land with Celestia, because it doesn't dismiss the Nursery as naïve — it inherits the Nursery's deepest concern and shows a better answer.
>
> From Applejack's POV, this is particularly resonant. Applejack has spent the whole story inside the argument. She can't hear it whole from the outside the way the reader can. The scene being from her perspective means the reader gets to watch Applejack hear, for the first time, a complete articulation of what she has been becoming — and watch it land on the one pony whose approval Applejack has been unconsciously seeking all along.
>
> After Twilight declares the Element of Conscience, there is silence for a moment with celestia's expression unreadable. Then Rainbow Dash bursts out laughing and calls Twilight an egghead because she can't help it and Applejack gets angry at Rainbow. Finally, Celestia chuckles as well and says something like "seems like you learned a very important lesson" in the style of Celestia's season 1 appearances. Then they negotiate the rules of the referendum 
>
> The purpose is to establish an in universe reason to lay out the meta connection. So Twilight saying it all in the language of the show is to make their "adulthood plans" comprehensible to their mother figure using the language of the show

**Threads:**
* **[[Discovering the Elements of Liberty]]**

**Themes:**
* **[[Conscience]]**

---
### SCENE 26.25: Round of debate with Celestia, then the Referendum is set
> **Synopsis:** Celestia can genuinely acknowledge that Twilight's synthesis is correct — that these are the elements of liberty, that Conscience is the sixth — while still believing that Equestrian ponies are not ready for a republic. Her position was never "Harmony is all you need." Her position, per the story plan, is that the Nursery was a specific response to a specific historical moment (the onset of the Griffonian Gilded Age) and that her ponies will follow the same arc as the griffons if unleashed too fast. The referendum tests whether she is right about her own ponies. Applejack says they are ready. Celestia says she's not certain.
> The "seems like you learned a very important lesson" line should feel like Celestia is grading the theory as correct while reserving judgment on the application. An Alicorn who has watched civilizations rise and fall for a thousand years does not change her assessment of readiness based on one very good speech. The tension of the election arc is not "will Celestia accept the argument" — the chuckle confirms she does. The tension is "will the ponies prove they are ready, in a compulsory vote where even the scared and the comfortable must choose."
>
> Celestia refuses to attack the changelings, saying how can harmony be the aggressor into a foreign country?
> Applejack says harmony is not the natural state of affairs, and must be fought for
>
> Celestia says the Griffonian Republic deposed the peaceful harmonic kingdom of Vedina. They are just the same old Herzland imperialists of the last 300 years
> Applejack says that's not true, I actually met Kemerskai, unlike you, who rejected his letter
> Applejack says they liberated Vedina, which otherwise would have been crushed by Chrysalis or Eros or the next warmongering Grover
> The former king of Vedina is still there, just an ordinary citizen. He never committed crimes against his griffons, so he is just a citizen now
>
> Celestia does not want magic to get into the wrong hooves
> How can you trust him?
> She is appalled that Applejack left their weapons, including draining crystals, canisters and magical rifles in the hands of the griffons
> They must stay isolationist
> Can't let someone invent something that will destroy the world (a nuke, just like Nightmare Moon's dream sucking spell)
> We can't sit back and let Chrysalis invent such a thing. We must achieve world peace by allying with the GR and AR
>
> You said Aquileia was just griffons abusing ponies. I said Aquileia was a dream of harmony between griffons and ponies where everyone gets along and uses industry to enhance their special talents
> Neither of us are right. Aquileia is messy. It's a balancing act between the urban industrialists, the rural farmers/artisans, and they leave everyone without an ego behind
>
> But we are not going to copy Aquileia's ego or the Griffonian Republic's standardization. We will combine them both with Equstrian Conscience
>
> Compulsory voting, no creature lives under a rock. No such thing as "turnout elections". Proportional representation like in France, it's copied from Aquileia
>
> I think Celestia will actually agree to a formal referendum vote after AJ points out that taking away what they have is tyrannical, because she is logical. And her estimated support is the highest. They agree on compulsory voting. Both of them think it will help them. Especially Celestia, since ponies who are scared should be looking up to her for a return to norm. Celestia also agrees that Temberik, Tzinacatl and Buffalo are also allowed to vote (actually, compulsory for them too) because she isn't a poser, just an overprotective mother
> It should be clear that pretty much none of Celestia's actions or decisions make her a poser, or at least not a majority. But, changeling POWs don't get to vote. AJ thinks that's a fair compromise.
>
> Ponies who don't vote are counted as a No for the Republic question
> If the republic passes, the final proportional representation will contain a party for the No voters
>
> The rules are: you vote for a party. If Celestial Party wins an outright majority, no Republic is formed. We return to the pre war government (Alicorn Rule/Celestial rule). If Celestial Party does not win an outright majority, a Republic is formed, and seats in parliament are assigned to the parties proportional to the vote. Laws need 50% vote to pass, and the president needs 50% support from the members. It's an exact copy of the Aquileian system, which is already fleshed out. Also, party line votes are not guaranteed, just like the Aquileian game mechanics (that's a monarchy way of thinking, anyway (the game text))
>
> However, the mixed member elections should not be held at the same time as the referendum. That would be unfair to the Celestial party, which won't have candidates ready. So the referendum is just about "do we become a Republic" and "if so, what will be the initial parties/blocs" of the Republic.
>
> If the Republic is born, parties need at least 5% proportion of the vote to get any seats, to prevent excessive fracturing. The referendum also serves to establish the pioneering parties, so that there is no excessive fracturing during the first mixed member election which, if the Republic is born, will happen a month later.
>
> I don't think current day France's extra layer of the 2 round presidential system makes sense to use, and it's not a thing in Aquileia (or the original French Republic?)
>
> Crystal Empire is excluded
> All thestrals are included
> Buffalo included too

---
## CHAPTER 27: Equity
> **Chapter Note:** The chapter "Equity" is about the spread of Harmonic Capitalism, repurposing the war economy into a harmonic abundance economy. Via Twilight's commoditized magic, good relations with the Temberik, changelings and buffalo, commoditized coal and oil industries, ponies (who already love each other!) learning how to have healthy sex (!) as a viable outlet of their newfound ambitions, weather on demand? Since democratized magic and industry make weather cheaper to produce instead of tying over half the pegasi population (by design, to pacify them after Hearth's Warming Eve era)
>
> I can't believe how well this complete narrative is going to work. Celestia and Luna are finally going to be actual, honest to goodness characters instead of pitied goddesses like my original plan!
> **Chapter Note:** Both Applejack and Celestia were overconfident. Is it confirmation bias or selection bias? Their biases meet reality at the first polling results.
> **Chapter Note:** Before, the narrative was laser focused on Applejack, Twilight and their close friends. Now we begin to see and get a retrospective of how the ponies of Equestria once viewed Applejack pre-war, how they viewed her during the war, and how they currently view her in the face of the referrendum
> **Chapter Note:** Remember when you asked me to bring Twilight back? I'm sorry I didn't
> [What would Celestia say or how would she react in response to this?]
> **Chapter Note:** With the new framing of the stagnation of harmony as only 80 years old and Celestia's changed psychology and character arc, what are the initial polling numbers that makes the most sense to deliver the themes?

### SCENE 27.1: Fluttershy talks to Celestia
> **Synopsis:** Fluttershy apologizes to Celestia for yelling at her. Celestia says it's okay.
>
> Remember when you asked me to bring Twilight back? I'm sorry I didn't
> [What would Celestia say or how would she react in response to this?]
>
> I stayed in Tall Tale to protect the animals
> But then it became CIA 
> Same with the POWs 
> They became infiltrators
> Fluttershy says she wishes she could just protect her animals from harm but that's not how the world works.
>
> Fluttershy is about to leave when Celestia says, Fluttershy? Can I tell you about an old friend?
> Fluttershy takes a seat next to Celestia
> He was a griffon named Henrik. (some wholesome details)
> He passed away around 100 years ago. History remembers him as Grover III the Unifier.
> Celestia says she did make friends with the thestrals
> It's hard for me to make new friends, Celestia admitted
>
> Surprisingly, Fluttershy says she understands that too! Because animals have shorter lifespans than ponies
>
> Celestia says she really regrets manipulating Discord now
> Fluttershy says it's never too late to apologize

**Characters:**
* **[[Princess Celestia]]**
> **Analysis:** The Death of the "Nursery" Fear: Celestia’s primary reason for the White Peace was the belief that exposure to the "Adult World" (friction, trauma, differing cultures) would turn ponies into monsters. The Thestrals prove this false. They navigate the darkest, most traumatic nightmares of Canterlot every night, yet they remain deeply empathetic, graceful, and grounded. They prove to Celestia that Resilience is a superior shield to Ignorance.

* **[[Fluttershy]]** (Impact: CoreValueDemonstration)
> **Analysis:** Fluttershy says she wishes she could just protect her animals from harm but that's not how the world works. She learned this lesson earlier in the buffalo chapter and again with the POWs!


**Themes:**
* **[[Elements of Liberty - Healthy Balance of Pink and Red]]**
> **Commentary:** The hint that Celestia couldn't wield the elements of harmony because of a lack of pink love, not a lack of red love

* **[[Deconstructing Democracy]]**
> **Commentary:** highly sophisticated use of Dramatic Irony. Revealing this conversation to the reader while hiding it from Applejack and Twilight does not lower the stakes; it actually elevates the thematic maturity of the entire final act.
> In a standard YA fantasy, the stakes are "Will the hero defeat the bad guy?"
> In a mature, materialist geopolitical drama, the stakes are "Will the society accept the burden of its own freedom?"
>
> The story ceases to be a rebellion against a corrupt system. Instead, it becomes a story of Civilizational Maturation. Applejack isn't overthrowing an evil empire; she is moving out of her parents' house. She acknowledges that the rules of the house kept her safe, but to survive in the adult world, she must write her own rules.


---

> **Synopsis:** Fluttershy tells Applejack it's time to rehabilitate Skyfall
> Applejack groans and says we can't do it yet, they are still selling to the Reich and Chrysalis
> Fluttershy gets angry, Applejack says we must save Aquileia first and we need the valves. I'm sorry, I haven't forgotten

### SCENE 27.3: Applejack visits her parents' factory

**Codex:**
* **[[EEEE!]]** (Organization)
> **Note:** They may operate in an Aquileian parloir, but their philosophy is fundamentally from the Griffonian Republic

* **[[Applejack's Parents]]** (Backstory)
> **Note:** Her parents give her the full story of how they went to Skyfall and wanted to establish the canning company to fix Skyfall's hunger. They saw impoverished griffons who couldn't fly. But they couldn't make a dent in the global market. Then they expanded so they can support the GR
>
> Applejack always thought their expansion was industrial greed, but it was charity that was going no where until they found a political purpose to support
>
> All the GR Riks they accepted as payment were used to expand the factories ahead of the Great War by buying standardized equipment from the GR. That's why their factories are so massive and "soulless" but supported the war effort

> **Synopsis:** This takes place at the meeting hall/rehab center

### SCENE 27.5: First polling results come in 45/30/25
> **Synopsis:** AJ had a lot of confidence. But the first broad polls come in (need to look into how statistical sampling works) and Celestial is at 45%, Harmonic Republicanism is at 30%, and Ponies First is at 25%. AJ is shattered. She didn't think Celestia would still be this popular. And the real tragedy is how many ponies are about to vote using hatred.
>
> The Silent Majority: Most ponies just want to go back to brunch. They are terrified of Applejack’s "New World" because it sounds like more work and more fighting.
>
> Ponies First Party is an alliance of convenience between ponies who want vengeance against changelings, ponies who are scared of other species, and ruthless industrialists who want isolationism and a protected domestic market while also exporting magical tech to the outside world for big profits (chaebol economy, I think?)
> Gilded Trust uses explosive rhetoric to get the attention of the ponies who don't like bat ponies/buffalo/crystal ponies and the ponies who are militant and want to shoot changelings
>
> How can Gilded Trust have nearly as much support as us?! Applejack cries
>
> Flowing Current says the workers of Las Pegasus are their own worst enemies. They reject collective bargaining (Unions) because they identify with the oppressor (The Boss). This makes liberating them frustrating for Applejack, adding texture to the world—you can save a pony from a changeling, but you can't save them from their own greed.
> The ponies in Vanhoover and Canterlot are hurt and want to hurt the changelings. They aren't thinking about progress or forgiveness, they are thinking about retribution
> They think Applejack is too soft and forgiving, despite her saving them
> These ponies lost family to the changelings
>
> For Celestia, they are traditionalists, older ponies, and those who are terrified of change. They want to go back to the golden age where the Princess solved everything. They are voting for a fantasy, but they don't understand reality, just that they put in everything to survive and want to go back to good times
>
> Applejack's coalition is all the volunteers in the army, EEEE and equivalent unions, buffalo and thestrals
>
> Stagnation doesn't just mean demobilization. It means Celestia wants to take away guns, love donators and red love canisters because it's an addictive drug (the entire magic economy), oil rigs (the entire industrial economy), doesn't want to integrate the Temberik, Buffalo or Tzinacatl (the non ponies who are born with ambition and it wasn't nurtured out), certainly doesn't want changeling POWs, who contributed to the war effort by donating the most of their own love, and Celestia basically wants ambition to go away. But just because she nurtured ambition away over a 1000 years doesn't mean she can put it back in a bottle now. Ponies do have ambition in their biology, it can't be nurtured away anymore once they have a taste of it. And the final nail is that Celestia wants to take away sex! Luna is in shambles.
>
> All the things necessary for war which the ponies got exposed to, and which could now be channeled for the harmonic capitalist economy, Celestia wants to take it all away. It's clear as day that a total return to the stagnation of harmony is clearly tyrannical to the harmonic capitalists. This is why they are up in arms. Celestia is conflating the harmonic capitalists with ruthless capitalists and the ponies who want retribution
>
> But the Celestial Party does have a plurality because ponies are traumatized from war and just want "not war economy" which is what Celestia promises.
>
> Initially, Applejack hated the screaming matches between the FJA and the PNdA. But under this new historical truth, she realizes that Democracy is Friction. The Aquileians yell at each other because they are taking adult responsibility for their society. The Equestrians were quiet because they were hiding in a crib. Applejack brings the "Noise" back to Equestria because noise means the ponies are finally awake.
> When Applejack and EEEE! push for the Equestrian Republic, they are not just changing the laws. They are forcing a species-wide psychological maturation.
> Applejack is telling the 85%: "Celestia can't protect you anymore. You cannot just write a petition and go back to sleep. You have to join a union. You have to vote. You have to hold a Luna Nova Rifle. You have to do the hard, messy work of protecting each other."
>
> This makes Gilded Trust's 25% "Ponies First" polling terrifyingly logical. When the Nursery walls fall, some ponies don't want to do the hard work of Harmonic Capitalism. They just want a new strongman to protect them. Gilded Trust offers to be their new, aggressive Father Figure. He promises to replace Celestia's "Soft Stagnation" with "Hard Supremacy," but the core appeal is the same: Submit to me, and I will solve the problem for you.
> **Outcome:** AJ is in shambles because she's nowhere close to a majority, and in the current state would be deadlocked by angry ponies and stagnant ponies. She must prove to every pony in every corner of the country that harmonic capitalism can work for them!

**Characters:**
* **[[Gilded Trust]]**
> **Analysis:** He is the leader of the Ponies First Party, patriot and ally turned new antagonist


**Threads:**
* **[[Stagnation's Redemption]]**
> **Update:** When the referendum first polling comes out, Applejack realizes it's not just Celestia's view point, it's 45% of Equestria's viewpoint too. But now she has an understanding of the hard work that needs to be done. If they had the referendum right away it would have been chaos
>
> Applejack has a mature understanding of what role the war played in their transformation 
> The hard work is already done: they already have a universal translator (harmony, plus they can trade for the physical devices), they have the industrial and magical capacity (from Chrysalis's engineering), the martial law and forced work phase is already over
> Now they just have to steer Equestria in the right direction

* **[[Temberik]]** (Organization)
